summary:
The Mesolithic colonization of Scandinavia followed closely behind the retreat of the [[event:1262|Laurentide and Fennoscandian ice sheets]] at the end of the last glacial period. By approximately 10,000–9,500 BCE, hunter-gatherer communities had moved northward along the Atlantic coast of Norway and into the newly deglaciated areas of southern Scandinavia, exploiting rich maritime and coastal resources including seal, whale, fish, and shellfish.

Early Scandinavian Mesolithic cultures include the Ahrensburg culture in Denmark and northern Germany (a late glacial reindeer-hunting tradition) and, from around 9000 BCE onward, the Maglemosian culture that occupied the forests and waterways of what are now Denmark, Sweden, and the adjacent lands of [[event:1319|Doggerland]]. Scandinavian Mesolithic communities developed sophisticated technologies for exploiting cold-water environments, including the manufacture of dugout canoes, fish weirs, and bark containers. The exceptionally well-preserved organic sites of Denmark and Norway — particularly in waterlogged coastal contexts — have yielded some of the richest collections of Mesolithic material culture in Europe.
